---
title: "TSA_Week2_PracticeQuestion4"
author: "Sumanth Kamath"
date: "2026-02-06"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## 

# TIME SERIES ANALYSIS LAB

## WEEK 2

### PRACTICAL 4

**For a given dataset of the US Population (in millions), write the R code to:**

(a) Import the data as a time series object.

(b) Identify the dominating component(s) in the data set.

(c) Apply a square root transformation to the original data set.

(d) Estimate the linear trend present in the data set

(e) Remove the estimated linear trend from the data set

    ![](Screenshot%202026-02-07%20093623.png)

### OBJECTIVE

The objective of this analysis is to import the given US Population dataset into the R environment and convert it into a time series object. The analysis includes identifying the dominant components of the time series, applying a square root transformation to stabilize variation, estimating the linear trend using regression, and removing the estimated trend to obtain a detrended series for further analysis.

### R - CODE

```{r}
# Command to delete all the R history
rm(list=ls(all=TRUE))
# To show the population in millions
options(scipen = 999, big.mark = ",")
```

```{r}
# Loading Required Libraries
library(datasets)
library(stats)
library(readxl)
library(forecast)
```

```{r}
# Importing the excel file containing US Population data
us_population <- read_excel("D:/SumanthKamath_Labs/SumanthKamath_230957214_TSA/Week2/us_population.xlsx")
View(us_population)
```

```{r}
# Importing the data as a time series object

# Data starts from 1970 and ends at 1990 (annual data)
us_pop_ts <- ts(us_population$Population,
                start = 1970,
                end = 1990,
                frequency = 1)
```

```{r}
# Plotting the time series graph
plot(us_pop_ts/1000000,
     main = "US Population Time Series",
     ylab = "Population (In Millions)",
     xlab = "Year",
     type = "o")
```

```{r}
# Applying a square root transformation to the original data set

us_pop_sqrt <- sqrt(us_pop_ts)
```

```{r}
# Plotting transformed data
plot(us_pop_sqrt,
     main = "Square Root Transformed US Population",
     ylab = "Sqrt(Population)",
     xlab = "Year",
     type="o")
```

```{r}
# (d) Estimating the linear trend present in the data set

# Create time index
time_index <- time(us_pop_ts)

# Fit linear trend model
trend_model <- lm(us_pop_ts ~ time_index)

# Display model summary
summary(trend_model)

# Estimated trend values
trend_estimate <- ts(trend_model$fitted.values, start=1970, frequency = 1)

# Plot original data with trend line
plot(us_pop_ts,
     main = "US Population with Linear Trend",
     ylab = "Population",
     xlab = "Year",
     type = "o")
lines(trend_estimate, col = "red", lwd = 2)
```

```{r}
# Removing the estimated linear trend from the data set

# Detrended series
us_pop_detrended <- us_pop_ts - trend_estimate

# Plot detrended data
plot(us_pop_detrended,
     main = "Detrended US Population Time Series",
     ylab = "Population (Detrended)",
     xlab = "Year",
     type = "o")
abline(h = 0, col = "blue", lty = 2)
```

### OBSERVATION

-   **Successful Data Import and Preparation**\
    The US population dataset was successfully imported from an Excel file using the `readxl` package and converted into a time series object using the `ts()` function. The data spans from 1970 to 1990 and represents annual observations, indicated by a frequency of 1.

-   **Nature and Frequency of the Time Series**\
    The dataset is an annual time series where each observation corresponds to the population value for a specific year. Since the frequency is 1, no seasonal component is expected in the data.

-   **Dominating Component**\
    The time series plot shows a strong increasing pattern over time, indicating that the **trend component** is dominant. There is no visible seasonal variation due to the yearly sampling frequency.

-   **Square Root Transformation**\
    A square root transformation was applied to the original dataset to reduce scale effects and stabilize variance. The transformed series retains the overall increasing pattern while compressing larger values.

-   **Linear Trend Estimation**\
    A linear regression model was fitted using time as the independent variable. The fitted trend line shows a steady increase in population over the years, confirming the presence of a strong linear trend component.

-   **Detrending the Series**\
    The estimated linear trend was removed from the original data to produce a detrended series. The resulting series fluctuates around zero, indicating that the main trend component has been successfully removed.

### CONCLUSION

The US population time series exhibits a strong upward linear trend, which is the dominant component of the dataset. Due to annual observations, no seasonal pattern is present. After applying a square root transformation and estimating the linear trend, the detrended series shows only minor irregular variations around the mean.
